title: Counting Rooms
description: You are given a map of a building with n rows and m columns. Each cell is either floor ('.') or wall ('#'). A room is a maximal connected component of floor cells (connected horizontally/vertically). Count the number of rooms.
input_spec: The first line contains two integers n and m (1 <= n, m <= 1000). Then n lines follow, each containing m characters ('.' or '#').

routes.name: Flood fill (DFS/BFS)
routes.description: Iterate over all cells. When you find an unvisited floor cell, run a flood fill (DFS or BFS) to mark the entire room, and increment the counter.

observations.title: Rooms = connected components
observations.description: Each room is a connected component of '.' cells. Counting rooms is counting connected components on a grid.

hints.nudge: What graph concept does 'group of connected floor cells' correspond to?
hints.question: If you think of each '.' cell as a node and adjacency as edges, what are you counting?
hints.formal: Rooms are connected components. Count them by iterating cells and running DFS/BFS from each unvisited '.' cell.

observations.title: Mark visited to avoid re-counting
observations.description: Use a visited array or modify the grid in-place (mark '.' as '#' after visiting) to avoid counting the same room twice.

hints.nudge: How do you ensure you don't count the same room more than once?
hints.question: After you flood fill a room, what should you do to those cells so you don't revisit them?
hints.formal: Maintain a visited[n][m] array, or mutate the grid: set visited cells to '#'. This ensures each cell is processed once.

observations.title: 4-directional neighbors
observations.description: Floor cells are connected horizontally and vertically (not diagonally). Use dx/dy arrays: [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)].

hints.nudge: How many neighbors does each cell have?
hints.question: Are diagonal cells considered connected? What directions should you check?
hints.formal: Use 4 directions: up, down, left, right. dx = [0,0,1,-1], dy = [1,-1,0,0]. Check bounds before accessing neighbors.

observations.title: Use iterative DFS to avoid stack overflow
observations.description: For grids up to 1000x1000, recursive DFS may cause stack overflow. Use an explicit stack or BFS with a queue.

hints.nudge: Think about the grid size. Could recursion be a problem?
hints.question: If the grid is 1000x1000 and one room fills the whole grid, how deep could your recursion go?
hints.formal: Max recursion depth could be 10^6, causing stack overflow. Use iterative DFS (explicit stack) or BFS (queue) instead of recursive DFS.
